results.title: HR Platform Buyer's Guide: How to Secure Executive Buy-in - Rippling
results.text:
HR Platform Buyer’s Guide: How to Secure Executive Buy-in | Rippling+

# HR platform buyer’s guide: How to secure executive buy-in for your next HR tech investment

## Unlock the real business impact of unified HR tech

Our guide reveals exclusive insights, frameworks, and proven methods from HR leaders who have successfully secured approval for major technology transformations:

The simple formula HR leaders use to calculate and present ROI to CFOs

How to frame your HR technology proposal in language that resonates with CEOs and COOs

The surprising risk of maintaining legacy systems — beyond just productivity loss

You didn’t get into HR leadership to chase paperwork or manually reconcile fragmented systems. But research shows that fragmented HR tech isn’t just an operational headache — it’s holding back strategic impact across your entire organization. The good news? HR leaders are successfully shifting the conversation from admin overload to strategic enablement — and so can you.

Getting executive buy-in for HR tech doesn’t happen by presenting a list of software features. It requires articulating the strategic outcomes that your leadership actually cares about. With this guide, you’ll learn how to clearly map your HR platform investment to compelling outcomes, such as:

Accelerating talent acquisition timelines

Dramatically reducing compliance risk exposure

Freeing hundreds of hours annually to redirect toward strategic initiatives that move the business needle

Discover how top HR teams successfully frame technology investments to secure the enthusiastic support of executives and stakeholders — and transform their impact from administrative to strategic.

## More HR resources

Tool3 terminations scripts and expert guidance

Video3 ways to access rich benefits and expert HR and compliance support

results.title: Ultimate HR System Switch Survival Guide - Rippling
results.text:
Ultimate HR System Switch Survival Guide | Rippling

Blog

- HR

# The ultimate HR system switch survival guide

Author

Author

Vanessa Kahkesh

Published

September 5, 2025

Updated

September 8, 2025

Read time

Read time

9 MIN

In this article

Why now (and why is your window closing)?

Make the case: Translate the switch into C-suite value

You got buy-in. Now what?

Make the switch without the stress

If you’re reading this, you’re probably running HR on a stack built out of “just get it done” decisions. It mostly works—until it doesn’t. The problem usually isn’t one bad tool. It’s the handoffs: the file feeds that break, the duplicated fields, the 8 p.m. “who owns this?” scramble before payroll. It’s not just messy—it’s risky.

But what if the risky move isn’t switching—it’s staying? Every month you hang onto that patchwork of disconnected tools, you pay for it: duplicate records, manual fixes, compliance risk, errors, and credibility with finance when payroll and the GL refuse to line up.

You don’t need a pep talk. You need a plan. This eight-week guide gives you one: a clear, realistic project plan to switch HR systems by Jan. 1 so your next year runs on just one platform.

Paige Erickson led Forbes Advisor through a global switch off five platforms, going live on Rippling with just an eight-week runway.

“Once we had buy-in and 100% sign-off, to move forward with Rippling, it was just under 10 weeks—maybe even eight—to get everything up and running for Jan. 1,” she said.

The switch was worth it to help Forbes Advisor go from processing five different payrolls on five different platforms every month to just one. And you can, too. If you want to start 2026 with one seamless, integrated HR system, this is your survival guide—and your step-by-step plan—to switch with confidence and start 2026 fresh.

Get the free template

## Why now (and why is your window closing)?

The sweet spot to select, implement, test, and cut over to a new HR and payroll system is spring through early fall. Right now—August—is still prime time.

You can technically switch any time of the year, but year-end cutovers are cleaner and less complex because it means starting the new year running payroll in one system. If payroll is split between two systems in January, employees’ W-2s and year-to-date tax records get fragmented across multiple providers, leading to tax complications and compliance risks.

But don’t wait until too close to the start of the new year. Here’s why a late Q4 cutover can put your team in a bind:

Open Enrollment collides with implementation.

If you start late, your team will have to configure a new system while managing plan changes and employee elections. The overlap multiplies risk and drains capacity when you need accuracy most.

Testing time gets squeezed.

Best practice when switching systems is to run test

results.title: How Do You Find the Perfect-Fit HR Tech? | Rippling+
results.text:
How Do You Find the Perfect-Fit HR Tech? | Rippling+

# How do you find the perfect-fit HR tech?

## Breakups don’t have to mean heartbreak

No one finds their Mr. or Mrs. Right without ending a relationship or two along the way. Too many companies settle for HRIS, payroll, and benefits administration software that’s wrong for them. But bad HR tech can slow your team down — while the right tech stack can allow your HR organization to thrive. Breaking up with the wrong HR software is the first step toward finding the right solution — and our guide will help you choose the right fit so this breakup can be your last.

## Today’s HR teams have more responsibilities than ever

But before you dive into comparing potential providers for HRIS, payroll, and benefits administration software, you’ll want to learn more about what you need from your tech stack for your HR organization to thrive.

83%

say they're expected to do more now compared to three years ago, so they need solutions to help them streamline their HR operations.

64%

say they lack time for most pressing compliance issues, so they need software to stay on top of local, state, federal regulations.

## More HR resources

Video3 ways to access rich benefits and expert HR and compliance support

ReportSapient Insights Group HR Systems Survey Report, 2025-2026

GuideThe HR Automation Maturity Framework

eBookWorkforce Advisory™

## See Rippling in action 

See how Rippling can help you manage all of your employee data and operations in one place, no matter your business's size.

See Rippling Watch tour video

results.title: How to evaluate an HRIS: RFP criteria and template | Rippling
results.text:
How to evaluate an HRIS: RFP criteria and template | Rippling

Blog

- HR

# Must‑have HRIS RFP template in 2025

Author

Author

Vanessa Kahkesh

Published

12 September 2025

Updated

16 September 2025

Read time

Read time

10 MIN

In this article

1. Employee system of record

2. Onboarding, offboarding, and lifecycle automation

3. Approvals, policies, and role‑based access

4. Time off and attendance

5. Benefits administration and employee experience

6. Payroll and compensation readiness

7. Integrations and APIs

8. Compliance and document management

9. Reporting and analytics

10. Global HR

How Rippling helps enterprises

Choosing a human resources information system (HRIS) today isn’t only critical to your HR organization—it’s what your entire business runs on.

Your system of record doesn’t just store employee data. It powers onboarding, approvals, access, payroll readiness, analytics, and compliance across HR, IT, and Finance. When the core is fragmented, teams chase spreadsheets and corrections; when it’s unified, work automates itself and leaders trust the numbers.

Use this guide to structure your RFP so you can separate demos from durable capabilities. For each area below, we explain why it matters, what good looks like, and the questions that help vendors prove it. Plus, we’ve put all the questions together in a downloadable template so you can start evaluating HRIS software quickly and effectively.

## 1. Employee system of record

Your employee record affects everything from payroll to provisioning and analytics. Your HRIS should model this.

It should capture real-world relationships (like manager, department, cost center, or legal entity), let you add custom fields, and control who sees what with clear role-based permissions. That way, the right people can get the info they need—without exposing data they shouldn’t.

Look for a unified employee record that keeps third‑party tools in sync automatically. For example, when someone changes their location, there should be updates to their payroll taxes and office access. Rippling does this natively, updating every connected system in real time so nothing slips through the cracks.

RFP questions to ask

Describe your core data model (employees, contractors, org, custom fields). How are relationships (manager, matrix) represented?

Can you enforce field‑level, role‑based permissions and scoped access for admins, managers, and HRBPs?

How do HRIS profile changes cascade to integrated apps (SCIM, attribute mapping, webhooks)?

What audit history, import/export, and bulk update capabilities are available?

How do you reconcile conflicting edits across systems?

## 2. Onboarding, offboarding, and lifecycle automation

Lifecycle moments are where risk and toil accumulate. Day‑one delays, missing
